## Plot Explinations
Plot 1: Distribution of Difficulty Ratings among Students With Prior Experience 
This plot allows us to easily see how experienced students rated the class' overall difficulty. We are looking for generally lower numbers in order to determine if experienced students are unchallenged by course content. This in turn shows whether or not they would even have a desire to look into future course content.

Plot 2: Distribution of Pace Ratings among Students With Prior Experience 
This plot is similar to the previous, except it looks into pace as opposed to difficulty. If experienced students find that the class is paced slowly, that gives us reason to believe they would benefit from being able to look ahead in course material.

Plot 3: Distribution of Prior Experience Among Freshman Students
For this plot, we are looking at how all of the freshman students answered the prior experience question. Since COMP110 is a beginner programming course, it is often taken by freshman studebnts. When discussing the prevelance of prior experience, we believe that an overview of the freshman class is most important, since those older. have more opportunitites to gain prior experience and skew the overall data. 

## Analysis
After our analysis, we have determined that the data does not show conclusive support for our idea. Our  idea of allowing students who have prior experience to view future course content was meant to benefit enrolled students as a stakeholder. However, for us to be certain of this benefit, we wanted to see conclusive evidence that their would be some desire for a policy such as this one among students. First of all, students with prior experience are within the minority among not only the entire population, but also specifically the freshman. Furthermore, through our graphs on difficulty and pace, we found that the vast majority of experienced students find the class to be moderately difficult, and generally well paced. If students with prior experience primarily answered that the class is very easy and paced slowly, we could reasonably conclude that they would greatly benefit from a chance to skip ahead in course content. Nevertheless, our analyses did not align with these expectations, which is why cement that this idea is not supported by the data.

## Conclusion 
While we were hoping for a different result, the analysis of the data is inconclusive in support of our idea. As mentioned in the analysis, if students with prior experiance found the class easy, it would be reasonable to assume more advanced course content would provide a more valuable and challanging experience. However, students with prior experience not only are a minority in the class, but also find the class moderatly challanging. While this doesn't refute our idea, it certianly can't support it as the speculation would have required experienced students to find the class easy.

A more direct, optional question specifically for advanced students would be needed for conclusivity. We would need to have a question that directly asks if students with prior experience would want the option to look ahead. This would be helpful because students finding the class moderate isn't an issue if they are ready and willing to move foward. This is especially true for early class because lots of concepts then are finniky, which contributes to the "moderate" tag even if it isn't a genuine challange. An important note is that it wouldn't technically discover if adding it would work. However, because the idea revolves around people, their support would warrent implementation. 

One cost would be the Professor's time, as they would need to update the Comp110 website to make this possible. There would also need to be some way such that only the intended students can see, or atleast some clarification that it's optional, which would most likley need to be spoken about in class. Another cost would be added work for TA's, as they would need to help with more advanced coursework. A potential tradeoff would be that students who did this would get better at more difficult concepts, but could maybe lose a grasp on the basics. Mainly, the negativly affected stakeholder would be the instructional staff for the reasons stated above.
